what: What are advanced baseball metrics and why do they matter?
title: Introduction to webgem
text: hitter’s offensive value based one skill. Two players can similar overall production getting different ways. One hitter may create value reaching base often, another may rely consistently hit ball hard. webgem designed help users compare different parts hitter’s offensive profile. OBP, -base percentage, measures often hitter reaches base hits, walks, hit--pitches. player strong OBP helps extend innings creates scoring opportunities, even always hitting power. SLG, slugging percentage, focuses power. Unlike batting average, slugging percentage gives extra credit doubles, triples, home runs. makes useful identifying hitters damage put ball play. player high SLG usually contributing extra-base hits rather singles. OPS (“-Base Plus Slugging”) combines OBP SLG, gives quick summary -base power, one reliable measured statistics used measure hitter’s overall offensive production. ’s meant combine well hitter can reach base, well can hit average power. wRC+ (“Weighted Runs Created Plus”) advanced statistic essentially measures hitter’s overall offensive value. places offensive production league-adjusted scale; value 100 represents league average, value 100 means hitter better league average. makes wRC+ helpful comparing hitters adjusts context better raw statistic like OPS. ISO, isolated power (ISO = SLG - BA), another advanced metric measures hitter’s raw power calculating many extra bases average per -bat. isolates player’s ability hit extra bases (doubles, triples, home runs) completely ignoring singles, showing whether hitter’s production coming extra-base hits. wOBA, weighted -base average, gives different offensive events different values. advanced statistic measures player’s overall offensive value assigning different run-values way player can reach base. Instead treating hits equally, properly weighs hits walks based actual contribution scoring runs. single, double, triple, home run, walk contribute equally run scoring. WAR, wins replacement, advanced, -encompassing statistic estimates total number wins player adds team compared freely available “replacement-level” player (e.g., minor leaguer bench player). Thus, attempts consolidate every facet player’s game (batting, base-running, fielding, pitching) one quantifiable number. Barrel percentage hard-hit percentage describe quality contact. broader terms, specific metrics tend focus final result play, purely well hitter hitting ball. high barrel percentage suggests player frequently making contact strong combination exit velocity launch angle. high hard-hit percentage solely suggests player consistently hitting ball high exit-velocity.

title: Introduction to webgem
text: specified baseball metrics can exceedingly useful, often use different scales. example, wRC+, OPS, OBP, SLG, WAR, barrel percentage useful, directly comparable raw form. wRC+ value might around 100, OPS value typically 1.000, barrel percentage measured percentage. challenge metrics measured differently. difficult objectively analyze full effect player’s offensive production (.e. multiple distinct metrics) without access quantitative forms comparison. webgem meant help users compare unique metrics.

title: Introduction to webgem
text: metricRank() takes numeric vector returns category labels based threshold values chosen user, turning numeric statistic readable category. user can decide label values low, middle, high. useful user wants classify statistic rather view raw number, comparing many players . column raw values can difficult scan quickly, column labels makes easier identify players fall performance group. function can used two ways. one threshold, creates two groups. example, user label every player OPS least 0.800 "strong OPS" everyone 0.800 ".800 OPS". function can also use two thresholds create three groups. case, values first threshold receive lower label, values two thresholds receive middle label, values upper threshold receive upper label. Overall, instead manually writing repeated if_else() statements time, user can quickly create readable performance groups numeric metric.
code: webgem_data$ops_rank <- metricRank(   x = webgem_data$ops,   threshold = 0.800,   lower_label = "below .800 OPS",   upper_label = "strong OPS" )  webgem_data[, c("player", "ops", "ops_rank")] webgem_data$ops_group <- metricRank(   x = webgem_data$ops,   threshold = 0.700,   upper_threshold = 0.850,   lower_label = "low OPS",   middle_label = "solid OPS",   upper_label = "excellent OPS" )  webgem_data[, c("player", "ops", "ops_group")]

title: Introduction to webgem
text: hitterProfile() creates profile table one hitter. user provides player name combination supported hitting metrics. function ignores metrics provided, user need fill every argument. example, user can select one player webgem_data pass player’s statistics hitterProfile(). output data frame metric name, original raw value, scaled value, player name. raw_value column keeps original baseball statistic. scaled_value column converts metric 0–100 scale metrics can compared easily. function useful prepares data comparison plotting. user can create smaller profile metrics fuller profile many metrics, depending want examine.
code: player_data <- webgem_data[webgem_data$player == "Shohei Ohtani", ]  ohtani_profile <- hitterProfile(   player = player_data$player,   wrc_plus = player_data$wrc_plus,   ops = player_data$ops,   obp = player_data$obp,   slg = player_data$slg,   avg = player_data$avg,   iso = player_data$iso,   war = player_data$war,   woba = player_data$woba,   bb_pct = player_data$bb_pct,   barrel_pct = player_data$barrel_pct,   hard_hit_pct = player_data$hard_hit_pct )  ohtani_profile #>   metric raw_value scaled_value        player #> 1   wRC+   151.000     67.33333 Shohei Ohtani #> 2    OPS     0.709     15.57143 Shohei Ohtani #> 3    OBP     0.335     49.23077 Shohei Ohtani #> 4    SLG     0.374     24.80000 Shohei Ohtani #> 5    AVG     0.246     42.66667 Shohei Ohtani #> 6    ISO     0.128     22.28571 Shohei Ohtani

title: Introduction to webgem
text: hitterRadar() takes profile data created hitterProfile() turns radar plot. radar plot uses scaled_value column, metrics shown 0–100 scale. function expects data frame least three important columns: metric, scaled_value, player. columns automatically created hitterProfile(), easiest workflow create profile first pass profile directly hitterRadar(). radar plot helps user see shape player’s offensive profile. Metrics farther center represent higher scaled values. makes easier notice whether hitter strongest power, -base ability, overall production, quality contact. typical workflow :  hitterProfile() organizes scales statistics, hitterRadar() visualizes scaled profile.
code: player_data <- webgem_data[webgem_data$player == "James Wood", ]  profile <- hitterProfile(   player = player_data$player,   wrc_plus = player_data$wrc_plus,   ops = player_data$ops,   obp = player_data$obp,   slg = player_data$slg,   avg = player_data$avg,   iso = player_data$iso, )  hitterRadar(profile)

what: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
title: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
text: webgem R package ranking, scaling, visualizing baseball hitting metrics. package designed make offensive statistics easier interpret turning raw numeric values clear labels, scaled hitter profiles, radar plots. , goal webgem provide users streamlined process interpreting metrics ranking, scaling, visualizing player statistics.

title: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
text: can install development version webgem GitHub :

title: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
text: package includes three main functions: metricRank() labels numeric values using one two user-defined thresholds. hitterProfile() creates scaled hitter profile selected offensive metrics. hitterRadar() creates radar plot hitter profile.

title: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
text: one threshold two thresholds
code: metricRank(   x = webgem_data$ops,   threshold = 0.8,   lower_label = "mediocre ops",   upper_label = "great ops" ) #>  [1] "mediocre ops" "great ops"    "mediocre ops" "great ops"    "mediocre ops" #>  [6] "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" #> [11] "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" "great ops"    "mediocre ops" "great ops"    #> [16] "mediocre ops" "great ops"    "mediocre ops" "great ops"    "great ops"    #> [21] "great ops"    "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" "mediocre ops" "great ops" metricRank(   x = webgem_data$ops,   threshold = 0.7,   upper_threshold = 0.9,   lower_label = "bad ops",   middle_label = "okay ops",   upper_label = "great ops" ) #>  [1] "okay ops"  "okay ops"  "okay ops"  "great ops" "okay ops"  "okay ops"  #>  [7] "okay ops"  "okay ops"  "bad ops"   "okay ops"  "bad ops"   "okay ops"  #> [13] "okay ops"  "okay ops"  "great ops" "okay ops"  "great ops" "okay ops"  #> [19] "okay ops"  "great ops" "okay ops"  "okay ops"  "bad ops"   "okay ops"  #> [25] "okay ops"

title: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics
code: player_data <- webgem_data[webgem_data$player == "Shohei Ohtani", ]  ohtani_profile <- hitterProfile(   player = player_data$player,   wrc_plus = player_data$wrc_plus,   ops = player_data$ops,   obp = player_data$obp,   slg = player_data$slg,   avg = player_data$avg,   iso = player_data$iso,   war = player_data$war,   woba = player_data$woba,   bb_pct = player_data$bb_pct,   barrel_pct = player_data$barrel_pct,   hard_hit_pct = player_data$hard_hit_pct )  ohtani_profile #>       metric raw_value scaled_value        player #> 1       wRC+   151.000     67.33333 Shohei Ohtani #> 2        OPS     0.709     15.57143 Shohei Ohtani #> 3        OBP     0.335     49.23077 Shohei Ohtani #> 4        SLG     0.374     24.80000 Shohei Ohtani #> 5        AVG     0.246     42.66667 Shohei Ohtani #> 6        ISO     0.128     22.28571 Shohei Ohtani #> 7        WAR     5.200     34.66667 Shohei Ohtani #> 8       wOBA     0.324     49.60000 Shohei Ohtani #> 9        BB%     8.900     34.70588 Shohei Ohtani #> 10   Barrel%     4.800     12.17391 Shohei Ohtani #> 11 Hard Hit%    28.100     20.25000 Shohei Ohtani

title: Tools for Ranking and Visualizing Baseball Hitter Metrics

what: Create a scaled hitter profile from user defined offensive metrics — hitterProfile
title: Create a scaled hitter profile from user defined offensive metrics — hitterProfile
text: function creates hitter profile selected offensive baseball metrics. user can enter combination offensive metrics listed, return data frame raw inputted values scaled values. scaled values can inputted hitterRadar function, plot metrics radar plot.

title: Create a scaled hitter profile from user defined offensive metrics — hitterProfile
code: hitterProfile(   player,   wrc_plus = NULL,   ops = NULL,   obp = NULL,   slg = NULL,   avg = NULL,   iso = NULL,   war = NULL,   woba = NULL,   bb_pct = NULL,   barrel_pct = NULL,   hard_hit_pct = NULL )

title: Create a scaled hitter profile from user defined offensive metrics — hitterProfile
text: player name player analyzed, must one character string. wrc_plus Weighted runs created plus. ops -base plus slugging. obp -base percentage. slg Slugging percentage. avg Batting average. iso Isolated power. war Wins replacement. woba Weighted -base average. bb_pct Walk percentage. barrel_pct Barrel percentage. hard_hit_pct Hard-hit percentage.

title: Create a scaled hitter profile from user defined offensive metrics — hitterProfile
text: data frame player name, selected metrics, raw inputted values, scaled values.

title: Create a scaled hitter profile from user defined offensive metrics — hitterProfile
code: judge_profile <- hitterProfile(   player = "Aaron Judge",   wrc_plus = 175,   ops = 1.110,   obp = 0.458,   slg = 0.701,   avg = 0.322,   iso = 0.379 )  judge_profile #>   metric raw_value scaled_value      player #> 1   wRC+   175.000     83.33333 Aaron Judge #> 2    OPS     1.110     72.85714 Aaron Judge #> 3    OBP     0.458     87.07692 Aaron Judge #> 4    SLG     0.701     90.20000 Aaron Judge #> 5    AVG     0.322     76.44444 Aaron Judge #> 6    ISO     0.379     94.00000 Aaron Judge

title: Create a radar plot from a hitter profile — hitterRadar
Create a radar plot from a hitter profile — hitterRadar
text: profile_data data frame created hitter_profile(), containing metric columns, scaled_value column, player column.

text: radar plot displaying chosen hitter's scaled offensive production

title: Create a radar plot from a hitter profile — hitterRadar
code: judge_profile <- hitterProfile(   player = "Aaron Judge",   ops = 1.000,   avg = 0.300,   slg = 0.700,   wrc_plus = 175,   iso = 0.210,   war = 9.5 )  hitterRadar(judge_profile)

what: Rank numeric values using one or two thresholds — metricRank
title: Rank numeric values using one or two thresholds — metricRank
text: function labels numeric values based user-defined threshold values. one threshold, values labeled lower upper. two thresholds, values labeled lower, middle, upper. function useful creating categories numeric metrics, especially preparing data summaries visualization.

title: Rank numeric values using one or two thresholds — metricRank
code: metricRank(   x,   threshold,   upper_threshold = NULL,   lower_label,   middle_label = NULL,   upper_label )

title: Rank numeric values using one or two thresholds — metricRank
text: x numeric vector label threshold cutoff value used separate lower values higher values. Values number receive lower_label, values receive upper_label. upper_threshold optional upper cutoff value. provided, values threshold upper_threshold receive middle label. lower_label label values threshold. middle_label label values threshold upper_threshold. used upper_threshold provided. upper_label label values upper cutoff/

title: Rank numeric values using one or two thresholds — metricRank
text: character vector labels

title: Rank numeric values using one or two thresholds — metricRank
code: era = c(2.03, 2.40, 5.49, 4.23, 3.46, 1.01, 4.50, 3.98, 3.76)  metricRank(   x = era,   threshold = 2.75,   upper_threshold = 3.75,   lower_label = "great era",   middle_label = "mediocre era",   upper_label = "bad era") #> [1] "great era"    "great era"    "bad era"      "bad era"      "mediocre era" #> [6] "great era"    "bad era"      "bad era"      "bad era"
